title: WHO Neuropsychiatric AIDS Study, Cross-sectional Phase II
abstract:
WHO Neuropsychiatric AIDS Study,
Cross-sectional Phase II
Neuropsychological and Neurological Findings
Mario Maj, MD, PhD; Paul Satz, PhD; Robert Janssen, MD; Michael Zaudig, MD; Fabrizio Starace, MD;
Lou D'Elia, PhD; Bhirom Sughondhabirom, MD; Mahamoudi Mussa, MD; Dieter Naber, MD; David Ndetei, MD;
George Schulte, MD; Norman Sartorius, MD, PhD
Background:Theneuropsychologicalandneurological com-
plications ofHIV-1 infection andAIDS were exploredwithin
the cross-sectionalphase oftheWHONeuropsychiatricAIDS
Study. Special attention was devoted to the controversial is-
sue of the prevalence and clinical significance ofsubtle cog-
nitive deficits in asymptomatic seropositive subjects.
Methods: Aneuropsychological testbattery validated for
cross-cultural use, a structured interview for the diagno-
sis of dementia, a rating scale of functioning in daily liv-
ing activities, and a neurological module were adminis-
tered to representative samples ofseropositive subjects and
to matchedseronegative controlslivingin the fivegeographic
areas predominantly affectedby the HIV-1 epidemic. Data
are available for five centers.
Results: The prevalence ofglobal neuropsychological im-
pairment wassignificantly increased in asymptomatic sero-
positive subjects compared with controls in only two cen-
ters. A significant effect of education on neuropsychologi-
cal performance was observed among asymptomatic sero-
positiveindividuals. Inthe twoAfricancenters, low-education,
but not high-education, asymptomatic seropositive persons
had an impaired performance. The frequency of impaired
functioningindailyliving activities and ofneurologic abnor-
malities was higher in symptomatic, but not in asympto-
atic, seropositive subjects compared with controls in all
centers.
Conclusions: These data suggest that the risk of subtle
cognitive deficits may be increased inasymptomatic stages
of HIV-1 infection. However, these deficits are not asso-
ciated with neurologic changes and do not seem to affect
subjects' social functioning.
(Arch Gen Psychiatry. 1994;51:51-61)
Several
GAPS still exist in the
currendyavailableknowledge
of the neuropsychological
complicationsofthe infection
produced by the human im¬
munodeficiency virus type 1 (HIV-1).
A first controversial issue is whether
subtle cognitive changes occur in a sig¬
nificant proportion of physically asymp¬
tomatic HIV-1-seropositive persons; in
fact, although many studies'"5 did not find
any significant difference in the perfor¬
mance on neuropsychological tests be¬
tween these subjects and matched HIV-
1-seronegative controls, several others6"10
found significant differences on mea¬
sures of attention, concentration, infor¬
mation-processing speed, and verbal
memory. The clinical significance of these
possible early mild abnormalities as pre¬
dictors of the future development of de¬
mentia also remains open to research; cur¬
rently available evidence11·12 suggests that
HIV-1-associated cognitive dysfunction
may remain stable for years, and it may
even improve spontaneously in some
cases,13 but further well-designed long-
term follow-up investigations, carried out
on samples representative of the broad
population of HIV-1-infected individu¬
als, are clearly needed. Another set of un¬
resolved issues concerns the relation¬
ships between HIV-1-associated cognitive
impairment and the immunological, medi¬
cal, neurological, and psychopathologi-
cal aspects of the infection: divergent find¬
ings have been reported, for instance,
concerning the correlation between neu¬
ropsychological performance and CD4

 SUBJECTS AND METHODS
SUBJECTS
The subject samples evaluated in the cross-sectional phase
of the WHO Neuropsychiatrie AIDS Study and the modali¬
ties of their enrollment are described in an accompanying
article.26
COGNITIVE/NEUROPSYCHOLOGICAL
ASSESSMENT
The module used for cognitive/neuropsychological assess¬
ment included (1) abriefquestionnaire on subjective com¬
plaints concerning cognitive functioning (with specific items
for memory, concentration, speech, and thinking); (2) a
battery of neuropsychological tests (see below); (3) an
adapted structured interview for the objective evaluation
of cognitive performance (SIDAM27), which incorporates
algorithms for the diagnosis of dementia according to the
International Classification ofDiseases, 10th Revision (ICD-
10)le and DSM-ÍII-R29; and (4) a rating scale of perfor¬
mance in activities ofdaily living (ADL), which collects in¬
formation from the subject and a key informant andprovides
individual scores for 17 items corresponding to different
ADLs, plus a global rating of disability.
The neuropsychological battery included the follow¬
ing tests: the Timed Gait; the Block Design subtest of the
Escala de Inteligencia Wechsler para Adultos (EIWA), ie,
the Spanish version of the Wechsler Adult Intelligence
Scale30; the Digit Symbol subtest of the EIWA; the Grooved
Pegboard, dominant and nondominant31; the Verbal Flu¬
ency Test, animals and first names32; the Trail Making A33;
the WHO/UCLA Auditory Verbal Learning Test; the Color
Trails 1 and 2; and the WHO/UCLA Picture Memory and
Interference Test (for the last four tests, see below). The
functional domains assessed by this battery are as follows:
( 1) motorspeed/fine motor control (Timed Gait, Color Trails
1 and 2, EIWA Block Design and Digit Symbol, Grooved
Pegboard, and Trail Making A); (2) sustained attention
(Color Trails 1 and 2 and Trail Making A); (3) selective
attention (Color Trails 2 and EIWA Digit Symbol) ; (4) cog¬
nitive flexibility (Color Trails 2 and EIWA Digit Symbol);
(5) perceptual-motor analysis (EIWA Block Design); (6)
verbal memory (WHO/UCLA Auditory Verbal Learning
Test) ; (7) visual memory (WHO/UCLA Picture Memory and
Interference Test); and (8) verbal fluency (Verbal Fluency
Test, animals and first names).
The tests included in the neuropsychological battery
were selected by an international committee of experts on
the basis of the following criteria: (1) ability to tap the func¬
tional domains that have been claimed to be affected in symp¬
tomatic HIV-1 infection, (2) sensitivity to mild degrees of
cognitive or motor dysfunction, (3) suitability for large-
scale administration, and (4) suitability for use in a cross-
cultural context. At the time ofits first meeting (July 1989),
the committee pointed out that no available test of either
verbal or visual memory fulfilled all the above criteria and
that the Trail Making B, which is a standard in HIV-1 re¬
search, was not suitable for cross-cultural use, due to the
presence of letters and written instructions. It was de¬
cided, therefore, to develop three new tests (one of verbal
memory, one ofvisual memory, and one replacing the Trail
Making B). These tests (the WHO/UCLA Auditory Verbal
Learning Test, the WHO/UCLA Picture Memory and In¬
terference Test, and the Color Trails) were validated by a
prepilot study.34
The WHO/UCLA Auditory Verbal Learning Test is simi¬
lar to the Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test,35 in that it is
a list-learning task. However, all ofits items have been care¬
fully selected (from categories such as parts of the body,
tools, household objects, and common transportation ve¬
hicles) to be familiar to a variety of cultures.
The Color Trails 1 and 2 have been developed follow¬
ing the idea of the Trail Making A and B. However, to mini¬
mize cultural bias, no letters or written instructions are used
in them. Both tests consist of several numbered circles col¬
ored in vivid pink or yellow: in Color Trails 1, each num¬
ber is represented by only one color, whereas in Color Trails
2, each number is printed twice—once in a pink and once
in a yellow circle. In Color Trails 1, the subject is in¬
structed to draw a line between the numbered circles one
after the other, following the number sequence. In Color
Trails 2, the subject must maintain the sequence of num¬
bers and alternate between pink and yellow.
The WHO/UCLA Picture Memory and Interference is
a completely new test, based on a large series ofline draw¬
ings representing a variety of objects familiar to most cul¬
tures. The test is presented visually with verbal instruc¬
tions. It consists of four sections called Books. In Book 1,
the subject is presented with a series of 20 drawings (tar¬
gets) printed on 3X5-in cards. He or she is instructed to
remember the target drawings. Immediately after, he or she
is presented with 50 more drawings and asked to indicate,
by saying yes or no, whether he or she saw each of the cards
in the target group. The recognition set includes 20 origi¬
nal targets and 30 distractors. In Book 2, the subject is pre¬
sented with 20 new targets that he or she is again in¬
structed to remember, followed by a 50-item recognition
trial. He or she is asked to identify with a yes response only
those cards that were targets in Book 2. Among the 50 rec¬
ognition items, there are 20 targets from Book 2, 10 new
distractors, 10 old distractors from Book 1, and 10 old tar¬
gets from Book 1 that now serve as distractors. In Book 3,
the subject is once again presented with 20 new targets that
are to be memorized, and then with another 50-item rec¬
ognition trial. Again, he or she is instructed to respond with
a yes only to those items that were targets in the most re¬
cent book. Among the 50 recognition items, there are now
20 targets from Book 3, 10 new distractors, five old distrac¬
tors from Book 1, five old distractors from Book 2, five old
targets from Book 1 that are now distractors, and five old
targets from Book 2 that are now distractors. In Book 4,

The subject samples evaluated in the cross-sectional phase
of the WHO Neuropsychiatrie AIDS Study and the modali¬
ties of their enrollment are described in an accompanying
The module used for cognitive/neuropsychological assess¬
ment included (1) abriefquestionnaire on subjective com¬
plaints concerning cognitive functioning (with specific items
for memory, concentration, speech, and thinking); (2) a
battery of neuropsychological tests (see below); (3) an
adapted structured interview for the objective evaluation
of cognitive performance (SIDAM27), which incorporates
algorithms for the diagnosis of dementia according to the
International Classification ofDiseases, 10th Revision (ICD-
10)le and DSM-ÍII-R29; and (4) a rating scale of perfor¬
mance in activities ofdaily living (ADL), which collects in¬
formation from the subject and a key informant andprovides
individual scores for 17 items corresponding to different
ADLs, plus a global rating of disability.
The neuropsychological battery included the follow¬
ing tests: the Timed Gait; the Block Design subtest of the
Escala de Inteligencia Wechsler para Adultos (EIWA), ie,
the Spanish version of the Wechsler Adult Intelligence
Scale30; the Digit Symbol subtest of the EIWA; the Grooved
Pegboard, dominant and nondominant31; the Verbal Flu¬
ency Test, animals and first names32; the Trail Making A33;
the WHO/UCLA Auditory Verbal Learning Test; the Color
Trails 1 and 2; and the WHO/UCLA Picture Memory and
Interference Test (for the last four tests, see below). The
functional domains assessed by this battery are as follows:
( 1) motorspeed/fine motor control (Timed Gait, Color Trails
1 and 2, EIWA Block Design and Digit Symbol, Grooved
Pegboard, and Trail Making A); (2) sustained attention
(Color Trails 1 and 2 and Trail Making A); (3) selective
attention (Color Trails 2 and EIWA Digit Symbol) ; (4) cog¬
nitive flexibility (Color Trails 2 and EIWA Digit Symbol);
(5) perceptual-motor analysis (EIWA Block Design); (6)
verbal memory (WHO/UCLA Auditory Verbal Learning
Test) ; (7) visual memory (WHO/UCLA Picture Memory and
Interference Test); and (8) verbal fluency (Verbal Fluency
Test, animals and first names).
The tests included in the neuropsychological battery
were selected by an international committee of experts on
the basis of the following criteria: (1) ability to tap the func¬
tional domains that have been claimed to be affected in symp¬
tomatic HIV-1 infection, (2) sensitivity to mild degrees of
cognitive or motor dysfunction, (3) suitability for large-
scale administration, and (4) suitability for use in a cross-
cultural context. At the time ofits first meeting (July 1989),
the committee pointed out that no available test of either
verbal or visual memory fulfilled all the above criteria and
that the Trail Making B, which is a standard in HIV-1 re¬
search, was not suitable for cross-cultural use, due to the
presence of letters and written instructions. It was de¬
cided, therefore, to develop three new tests (one of verbal
memory, one ofvisual memory, and one replacing the Trail
Making B). These tests (the WHO/UCLA Auditory Verbal
Learning Test, the WHO/UCLA Picture Memory and In¬
terference Test, and the Color Trails) were validated by a
The WHO/UCLA Auditory Verbal Learning Test is simi¬
lar to the Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test,35 in that it is
a list-learning task. However, all ofits items have been care¬
fully selected (from categories such as parts of the body,
tools, household objects, and common transportation ve¬
hicles) to be familiar to a variety of cultures.
The Color Trails 1 and 2 have been developed follow¬
ing the idea of the Trail Making A and B. However, to mini¬
mize cultural bias, no letters or written instructions are used
in them. Both tests consist of several numbered circles col¬
ored in vivid pink or yellow: in Color Trails 1, each num¬
ber is represented by only one color, whereas in Color Trails
2, each number is printed twice—once in a pink and once
in a yellow circle. In Color Trails 1, the subject is in¬
structed to draw a line between the numbered circles one
after the other, following the number sequence. In Color
Trails 2, the subject must maintain the sequence of num¬
bers and alternate between pink and yellow.
The WHO/UCLA Picture Memory and Interference is
a completely new test, based on a large series ofline draw¬
ings representing a variety of objects familiar to most cul¬
tures. The test is presented visually with verbal instruc¬
tions. It consists of four sections called Books. In Book 1,
the subject is presented with a series of 20 drawings (tar¬
gets) printed on 3X5-in cards. He or she is instructed to
remember the target drawings. Immediately after, he or she
is presented with 50 more drawings and asked to indicate,
by saying yes or no, whether he or she saw each of the cards
in the target group. The recognition set includes 20 origi¬
nal targets and 30 distractors. In Book 2, the subject is pre¬
sented with 20 new targets that he or she is again in¬
structed to remember, followed by a 50-item recognition
trial. He or she is asked to identify with a yes response only
those cards that were targets in Book 2. Among the 50 rec¬
ognition items, there are 20 targets from Book 2, 10 new
distractors, 10 old distractors from Book 1, and 10 old tar¬
gets from Book 1 that now serve as distractors. In Book 3,
the subject is once again presented with 20 new targets that
are to be memorized, and then with another 50-item rec¬
ognition trial. Again, he or she is instructed to respond with
a yes only to those items that were targets in the most re¬
cent book. Among the 50 recognition items, there are now
20 targets from Book 3, 10 new distractors, five old distrac¬
tors from Book 1, five old distractors from Book 2, five old
targets from Book 1 that are now distractors, and five old
targets from Book 2 that are now distractors. In Book 4,
the subject is presented with 15 items that appeared as tar¬
gets in the preceding books, and is instructed to indicate
whether the item was originally seen as a target in Book 1,
The researchers involved in neuropsychological assess¬
ment in all the centers were trained in the use of the test bat¬
tery by means of a workshop held in Nairobi, Kenya, in March
1990. Further details on this training, as well as on the tests
composing the battery, can be found in a previous article.22
The SIDAM was adapted for use in a cross-cultural con¬
text by amending some items (a copy of this international
version is available on request from WHO headquarters).
The high Intercenter and intracenter reliability in the use of
this instrument is reported in the above-mentioned prelimi¬
The ADL rating scale was constructed ex novo with
items appropriate to a cross-cultural context. Its suitabil¬
ity and acceptability were proved by the pilot investigation
preceding the present cross-sectional phase of the WHO
Neuropsychiatrie AIDS Study.22
The module used for neurological assessment included
the following parts: (1) a screening neurological examina¬
tion (including 11 sections: level of consciousness, cranial
nerves, antisaccadic eye movements, muscle strength, co¬
ordination, involuntary movements, reflexes, sensory,
Romberg test, gait, and other abnormalities); (2) a de¬
tailed neurological examination (including six sections:
cranial nerves, involuntary movements, tone, motor, co¬
ordination, and sensory); (3) a neurological history; and
(4) a neurological summary. A manual of instructions was
produced that is available on request from WHO head¬
quarters. Neurological diagnoses were made according to
the operational criteria proposed by the WHO16 and by
the American Academy of Neurology (AAN), Minneapo¬
lis, Minn.36 The training of the researchers and the inter-
center and intracenter reliability data are reported in the
above-mentioned preliminary article.22
The asymptomatic and symptomatic HIV- 1-seropositive sub¬
jects evaluated in each center were compared separately with
the HIV- 1-seronegative controls with respect to the preva¬
lence ofimpairment on individual neuropsychological tests
and of global neuropsychological impairment. Impair¬
ment on each test was defined as a performance 2 or more
SDs worse than the mean of the seronegative group. Glo¬
bal impairment was defined as an impaired performance
on at least three of 10 tests (impairment on one or both
Grooved Pegboard tests or on one or both Verbal Fluency
tests was counted as one impaired test). A comparison with
regard to the prevalence of global neuropsychological im¬
pairment was also performed between high-education
and low-education asymptomatic and symptomatic HIV-1-
seropositive subjects and the corresponding subgroups ofHIV-
1-seronegative controls: high-education subjects were those
whose number ofyears ofeducation was higher than the 25th
percentile of the frequency distribution in the entire sample
recruited in that center. All the above comparisons were made
by using the  2 test or the Fisher's Exact Test, as appropri¬
ate. A Bonferroni correction for multiple testing was made.
In comparing the prevalence of global neuropsychological
impairment in each seropositive group with that of serone¬
gative controls in each center, the possible confounding ef¬
fect ofdepressive symptoms was evaluatedby calculating the
odds ratios (ORs) in the two subgroups obtained by strati¬
fying for the global score on the Montgomery-Asberg De¬
pression Rating Scale (MADRS37) (cutoff=19/20) and by ap¬
plying Woolfs test for OR heterogeneity.38
A stepwise logistic regression analysis39 was per¬
formed separately in asymptomatic and symptomatic HIV-
1-seropositive subjects (of all centers lumped together) by
using global neuropsychological performance as the de¬
pendent variable, whereas independent variables were cen¬
ter, sex, age, education (years ofschooling), history ofhead
traumas (causing loss of consciousness and requiring hos-
pitalization), current use ofanxiolytics, history of malaria,
history of syphilis, CD4 count, current and lifetime psy¬
chiatric diagnosis (according to either 1CD-10 or DSM-IIÍ-
R), and presence of prominent depressive symptoms (ie, a
score of at least 20 on the MADRS). Treatment with zi-
dovudine and current use of opiates were not included be¬
cause they overlapped almost completely with the "cen¬
ter" variable. The same logistic regression analysis was also
performed in HIV-1-seronegative and asymptomatic HIV-
1-seropositive subjects of all the centers by using global
neuropsychological performance as the dependent vari¬
able and all the above-mentioned parameters plus HIV-1
serostatus as independent variables. In all logistic regres¬
sion analyses, the adjusted ORs with 95% confidence in¬
tervals (CIs) were calculated for all dichotomous indepen¬
dent variables that entered the models. Correlations of
neuropsychological test scores with the CD4 count and
MADRS global score were explored in each seropositive
group by the Pearson coefficient.
The asymptomatic andsymptomatic HIV- 1-seropositive
individuals evaluatedin each center werecomparedseparately
with HIV- 1-seronegative controls with respect to the preva¬
lence ofsubjective cognitive complaints, the mean total score
on the ADL rating scale, and the prevalence of a diagnosis of
dementia according to ICD-10 and DSM-IÍÍ-R. Comparisons
were madeby using the Scheffé test for continuous measures
and the  2 test (with the Bonferroni correction for multiple
testing) for categorical measures. Associations between the
occurrence ofat least one subjective cognitive complaint, on
the one hand, and the presence ofglobal neuropsychological
impairment and ofprominent depressive symptoms, on the
other, were assessed in each serogroup by the  2 test.
The asymptomatic and symptomatic HIV-1-
seropositive subjects assessed in each center were com¬
pared separately with HIV-1-seronegative controls
with respect to the prevalence of impairment on in¬
dividual sections of the neurological examination and
the prevalence of current neurological diagnoses ac¬
cording to WHO and AAN criteria. Comparisons were
made by using the Scheffé test for continuous mea¬
sures and the  2 test (with the Bonferroni correction
for multiple testing) for categorical measures.
The  2 test was used to assess, in each serogroup,
associations between the occurrence of impairment
on each section of the neurological examination, on
the one hand, and the presence of global neuropsy¬
chological impairment, ofprominent depressive symp¬
toms, and ofany ICD-10 or DSM-ÍÍÍ-R psychiatric di¬
agnosis, on the other. The Scheffé test was applied in
each seropositive group to compareneurologically im¬
paired vsnonimpairedsubjectswithrespect to the mean
CD4 countandmeantotal score on theADLrating scale.
count,2'3or the impact of depression on cognitive func¬
tioning,14·15 in HIV-1-seropositive individuals. Finally, al¬
though several estimates of the prevalence of HIV-1-
associated dementia have been produced,16 they are
hampered by the very rare utilization of structured di¬
agnostic interviews and operational diagnostic criteria.
A more general limitation of research concerning
HIV-1-associated cognitive impairment is that available
studies have been almost exclusively conducted on
samples of well-educated, mostly white, homosexual
men in Western industrialized areas. Reports from de¬
veloping countries, in this research field, are very few,
and the findings from these reports are inconsistent.
For example, concerning the prevalence of dementia
among patients with acquired immunodeficiency syn¬
drome (AIDS) in sub-Saharan Africa, figures as high as
54%17 and as low as 3.2%18 can be found in the litera¬
ture. These findings hardly can be compared with each
other and with those obtained in Western countries,
owing to huge differences in population-sampling pro¬
cedures and assessment methods.19
And yet, there are reasons to suspect that interre¬
gional differences in the prevalence and the natural his¬
tory ofHIV-1-associated neuropsychological changes do
exist. It is possible that subtle cognitive deficits become
apparent earlier in HIV-1-infected subjects living in some
developing countries because their "cerebral reserve" (ie,
redundancy ofcerebral networks) may be reduced by or¬
ganic or psychosocial factors (eg, malnutrition, other in¬
fectious diseases affecting the central nervous system, and
lack of social and educational stimulation).20'23 On the
other hand, progression to severe cognitive impairment
may be prevented, in the same contexts, by early death
due to wasting syndrome or opportunistic infections, so

WSignificant intracenter differences (asymptomatic or symptomatic seropositive vs seronegative subjects): P<.05.
that the prevalence of the dementia syndrome may be
found to be lower than in Western countries.24
The very rare utilization ofstandardized assessment
and diagnostic instruments and the shortage ofstudies con¬
ducted in developing countries are also evident in the lit¬
erature onneurologicalcomplications ofHIV-1 infection.
This insufficiency ofreliable empirical data has generated
contrastingviews, such as the one expressed in theWorld
Health Organization (WHO) Bangui clinical definition of
AIDS23 (according to which neurological impairment is
just a "minor" manifestation ofHIV-1 infection inAfrica)
and that contained in a recent article by Howlett et al17 (ac¬
cording to which "neurological disorders are among the
main clinical features ofAIDS and HIV disease inAfrica").
Within the cross-sectional phase of the WHO Neu¬
ropsychiatrie AIDS Study, we assessed the prevalence of
HIV-1-associated neuropsychological and neurological
abnormalities in representative samples of HIV-1-
seropositive subjects living in the five geographic areas
predominantly affected by the HIV-1 epidemic. Herein,
we report the results of this assessment.
As asymptomatic HIV-1-seropositive subjects were com¬
pared with HIV-1-seronegative controls with respect to
the prevalence of impairment on individual neuropsy¬
chological tests, only a few significant differences were
observed. On the contrary, symptomatic HIV-1-
seropositive individuals differed significantly from con¬
trols on numerous neuropsychological measures (their
performance was significantly worse on Timed Gait, Color
Trails 2, EIWA Digit Symbol, Trail Making A, and Ver¬
bal Fluency, animals, in three centers) (Table I ).
The prevalence ofglobal neuropsychological impair¬
ment was significantly higher in symptomatic HIV-1—
seropositive subjects than in controls in all the centers. A
similar significant difference was found between asymp¬
tomatic HIV-1-seropositive subjects and HIV-1-
seronegative individuals in Säo Paulo, Brazil, and Kin¬
shasa, Zaire, but not in the other centers. In Kinshasa and
in Nairobi, the prevalence of global neuropsychological
impairment was significantly higher in low-education, but
not in high-education, asymptomatic HIV-1-seropositive
subjects compared with the corresponding subgroups of
HIV-1-seronegative individuals (Table 2). Woolfs test
for OR heterogeneity provided no evidence of multipli¬
cative interactionbetween HIV-1 seropositivityandMADRS
global score in any of the above comparisons.
On stepwise logistic regression analysis, performed
separately in asymptomatic and symptomatic seroposi¬
tive subjects, the following variables entered the model:
education in the former group (improvement  2=17.9,
d/=l, P<.00001; 81.7% of subjects correctly classified;
OR=6.49 [CI, 2.46 to 7.12]); age (improvement  2= 14.0,
df=l, P<.001); lifetime psychiatric diagnosis (improve¬
ment  2=10.2, df=l,P<.01); center (improvement  2=12.8,
dj=4, P<.05); depressive symptoms (improvement  2=7.1,

df=l,P<.01); and sex (improvement x2=5.0,d/=l,P<.05)
in the latter group (83.2% ofsubjects correctly classified
in the final model; OR=3.33 [CI, 1.40 to 7.85] for life¬
time psychiatric diagnosis, and OR=2.89 [CI, 1.20to6.96]
for depressive symptoms). On the stepwise logistic re¬
gression analysis performed in seronegative and asymp¬
tomatic seropositive subjects, the variables entering the
model were education (improvement  2=17.0, df=l,
P<.00001) and HIV-1 serostatus (improvement  2=10.2,
df=l, P<.01) (95.1% ofsubjects correctly classified in the
final model; OR=3.81 [CI, 1.79 to 8.85] for education,
and OR=3.66 [CI, 1.52 to 8.85] for serostatus). When
the educationXserostatus interaction was run as an in¬
dependent variable, it entered the model with an im¬
provement  2 of 31.7 (d/=l, P<.00001; 95.1% of sub¬
jects correctly classified; OR=8.58 [CI, 4.01 to 18.36]).
Only a few significant correlations between neuro¬
psychological test scores and the CD4 count were observed
seropositivepersons. The most frequentlyinvolved tests were
Color Trails 1 (P<.01 among asymptomatic subjects in
Nairobi; P<.05 among both asymptomatic and symptom¬
atic persons inMunich, Germany) and ColorTrails 2 (P<.01
among asymptomatic subjects in Munich; P<.05 among
symptomatic individuals in Munich and Kinshasa).
*ADLs indicates activities of daily living; HIV-1, human
immunodeficiency virus type 1; HIV-1-, HIV type 1-seronegative subjects;
A/HIV-U, asymptomatic HIV type 1-seropositive subjects; and S/HIV-U,
symptomatic HIV type 1-seropositive subjects.
^Higher scores indicate worse performance.
^Significant intracenter differences (symptomatic seropositive vs
seronegative subjects): P<.05.
Significant correlations between neuropsychologi¬
cal test scores and the MADRS global score were more fre¬
quent, especiallyinsymptomaticseropositive subjects. The
most frequently involved tests were Color Trails 1 (P<.01
among both asymptomatic and symptomatic subjects in
Munich and symptomatic persons in Säo Paulo; P<.05
amongsymptomaticsubjects in Nairobi andBangkok, Thai¬
land), EIWA Digit Symbol (P<.01 among asymptomatic
individuals in Bangkok; P<.05 among both asymptom¬
atic and symptomatic subjects inMunich and among symp¬
tomatic persons inBangkok), and GroovedPegboard, domi¬
nant (P<.001 among asymptomatic subjects in Munich;
P<.01 amongsymptomatic individuals in Munich and Kin¬
shasa; and P< .05 amongasymptomaticsubjects inNairobi).
The proportion ofsymptomatic seropositive subjects
presenting at least one subjective cognitive complaint was
significantly higher than that of seronegative persons in
all the centers (12.2% vs 0%, P<.01 in Munich; 17.4% vs
3.9%, P<.05 in Sao Paulo; 21.8% vs 1.2%, P<.01 in Kin¬
shasa; 15.3%vs3.1%, P<.05 inNairobi; and 15.8%vs3.4%,
P< .05 in Bangkok). A similar difference between asymp¬
tomatic seropositive subjects and controls was detected
only in Kinshasa (9.6% vs 1.2%, P< .05). The most frequent
subjective complaints in symptomatic seropositive sub¬
jects were those concerning memory disturbances, con¬
centration problems, and slowness ofthinking (the preva¬
lence ofeach of these complaints was significantly higher
in these subjects than in seronegative controls in three of
five centers). There was no significant difference between
asymptomatic seropositive subjects and controls with re¬
spect to the prevalence ofindividual subjective complaints.
The presence of at least one subjective cognitive com¬
plaint was significantly associated with global neuropsy¬
chological impairment in symptomatic seropositive sub¬
jects assessed in Munich (P< .01) and Säo Paulo (P< .001),
whereas no significant association was found in asymptom¬
aticseropositive subjects in any center. The association be¬
tween the occurrence of at least one subjective cognitive
complaint and the presence ofprominentdepressive symp¬
toms was significant in asymptomatic seropositive subjects
enrolled inMunich (P<.01), Kinshasa (P< .001), andNairobi
(P<.05), and in symptomatic seropositive individuals as¬
sessed in Kinshasa (P<.01) and Nairobi (P<.05).
The mean total score on the ADL rating scale was
significantly higher in symptomatic HIV-1-seropositive
subjects than in controls in all the centers, and it was con¬
sistently not increased in asymptomatic HIV-1-
seropositive subjects (Table 3). Actually, none of the
17 items of the ADL rating scale explored in the five cen¬
ters showed any significant difference between asymp¬
tomatic HIV-1-seropositive and HIV-1-seronegative
No case of dementia according to either 1CD-10 or
DSM-1II-R was detected in HIV-1-seronegative and
asymptomatic HIV-1-seropositive persons in any of the
centers. The prevalence of the syndrome in symptomatic
HIV-1-seropositive patients ranged from 5.4% to 6.9% ac¬
cording to 1CD-10 criteria and from 4.4% to 6.5% accord¬
ing to DSM-IÏÏ-R criteria in all the centers except Bangkok,
where no case of dementia was diagnosed (Table 4).
The prevalence of neurological impairment was signifi¬
cantly higher in symptomatic (but not in asymptomatic)
HIV-1-seropositive individuals, compared with sero¬
negative controls, in all the centers. The most frequently
involved items ofthe neurological examination were fron¬
tal release signs (significant difference between symptom¬
atic seropositive subjects and controls in all the centers),
antisaccadic eye movements (significant differences in three
centers), and muscle strength (same as antisaccadic eye
movements). Of 65 item comparisons in the five centers,
no one showed a significant difference between asymp¬
tomatic seropositive subjects and controls (Table 5).
Predominantly sensory neuropathy was the most com¬
mon neurological diagnosis in symptomatic HIV-1-
seropositive subjects, and it occurred in one physically
asymptomatic HIV-1-seropositive subject, both in Sào Paulo
and in Bangkok. Inflammatory polyneuropathy was the
other diagnosis sporadically made (one case in Munich)
in the asymptomatic seropositive group. Myopathy was very
rare also in symptomatic seropositive subjects (one case
in Munich and one case in Bangkok), as was acute inflam¬
matory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy diag¬
nosed according to AAN criteria (one case each in Mu¬
nich, Kinshasa, and Nairobi) (Table 6). No neurological
diagnosis was made in HIV-1-seronegative subjects.
In all the centers, impairment on each section of neu¬
rological examination was not significantly associated,
in any serogroup, either with the presence of prominent
depressive symptoms or with any ÍCD-10 or DSM-III-R
psychiatric diagnosis, whereas several associations were
found between the occurrence ofindividual abnormal neu¬
rological items and the presence of global neuropsycho¬
logical impairment in symptomatic (but not in asymp¬
tomatic) seropositive subjects (Table 7). As
neurologically impaired symptomatic seropositive sub¬
jects were compared with nonimpaired ones, the former
had a significantly decreased mean CD4 count in Mu¬
nich and Nairobi and a significantly increased mean to¬
tal score on the ADL rating scale (that is, a worse per¬
formance in ADLs) in Munich and Kinshasa. No significant

difference with respect to the mean CD4 count and mean

neurologically impaired and nonimpaired asymptom¬
The results of the cross-sectional phase of the WHO Neu¬
ropsychiatrieAIDSStudyconfirm thatthesymptomaticstages
ofHIV-1 infection are characterizedby asignificantimpair¬
ment of cognitive functions. This finding holds in outpa¬
tientsamples, excluding the most severe cases ofAIDS. Fine
motor control, sustained and selective attention, cognitive
flexibility, and verbal memory are the most frequently af¬
fected functional domains, and the pattern ofimpairment
does not seem to differ in the variousgeographical contexts.
Cognitive impairment is associatedwith a significant wors¬
ening of the performance in daily living activities.
Findings concerning physically asymptomatic HIV-
1-seropositive subjects are more ambiguous. Of 60 test
comparisons performed in the five centers, only three
showed a significant difference between asymptomatic se¬
ropositive and seronegative subjects. Nevertheless, the
prevalence of global neuropsychological impairment was
significantly higher in asymptomatic HIV-1-seropositive
persons than in controls in two centers, and HIV-1 se¬
rostatus was one of the variables affecting global neuro¬
psychological performance in the stepwise logistic regres¬
sion analysis performed for seronegative and asymptomatic
seropositive subjects of all the centers. Anyway, it is im¬
portant to stress that the performance in daily living ac-
tivities ofasymptomatic seropositive subjects was not sig¬
nificantly impaired in any center.
The "cerebral reserve" theory is partially supported by
the data collected. Within two centers in developing coun¬
tries (Kinshasa and Säo Paulo), the prevalence of neuro¬
psychologicalimpairmentwassignificantlyhigher in asymp¬
tomatic HIV-1-seropositive subjects than in HIV-1-
seronegative controls, and also the corresponding Nairobi
data approached statistical significance. Moreover, low-
education, but nothigh-education, asymptomaticseroposi¬
tivepersonshad animpairedneuropsychologicalperformance
in the two African sites, and a significant effect of the edu¬
cation level on the prevalence of the impairment in asymp¬
tomatic seropositive subjects ofall the centers was detected
by the stepwise logistic regression analysis. This lastfinding
confirms thepreliminaryevidence obtained in the Multicenter
AIDS CohortStudy.40Inadequateeducational stimulationmay
in itself be a factor affecting "cerebral reserve" (by limiting
the development ofefficient synaptic connections between
neurons), but a low-education level may also be seen as an
indicatorofa socioenvironmental situationwhere other fac¬
tors reducing the redundancy ofcerebral networks (such as
other infectious diseases and malnutrition) are at work.
Cognitiveimpairment was not found to besignificantly
related to immunological status: in fact, only very few cor¬
relations were observed between neuropsychological test
scores and the CD4count, eitherinasymptomatic orinsymp¬
tomatic HIV- 1-seropositive subjects, and no significant ef¬
fectof the CD4 count on global neuropsychological perfor¬
mance was revealedby thestepwiselogisticregressionanaly¬
sis. However, the question of the relationship between
cognitive and immunological decline will be more appro¬
priately addressedby the follow-up phase ofthe present in¬
Our findings indicate that the presence of promi¬
nent depressive symptoms is significantly associated with
the occurrence of subjective cognitive complaints, espe¬
cially in asymptomatic seropositive subjects. On the other
hand, subjective cognitive complaints of asymptomatic
seropositive persons do not correlate significantly to the
objective neuropsychological performance (in line with
findings from a previous report by van Gorp et al41). It is
essential, therefore, that any definition of HIV-1-
associated "mild cognitive impairment" require an ob¬
jective test validation: the operational diagnostic criteria
*HIV-1 indicates human immunodeficiency virus type 1; A/HIV-U, asymptomatic HIV type 1-seropositive subjects; S/HIV-U, symptomatic HIV type
1-seropositive subjects; WHO, World Health Organization (Geneva, Switzerland); and AAN, American Academy of Neurology (Minneapolis, Minn). No
neurological diagnosis was made in HIV type 1-seronegative subjects.
*NP indicates neuropsychological; HIV-1, human immunodeficiency
virus type 1; S/HIV-U, symptomatic HIV type 1-seropositive subjects; MU,
Munich, Germany; SP, Sao Paulo, Brazil; Kl, Kinshasa, Zaire; NA, Nairobi,
Kenya; and BG, Bangkok, Thailand.
recently produced by the WHO42 and by the AAN36 for
HIV-1-associated minor cognitive/motor disorder may
be a first step in this direction.
The results of the present study show that the preva¬
lence of dementia in the symptomatic stages of HIV-1 in¬
fection is almost the same in developed and in developing
countries, with the exception of Thailand (where the ex¬
plosionoftheepidemic has been morerecent, andmostHIV-1
cases are notlikely to have reached an advanced stage). The
nonincreased prevalence of dementia in sub-Saharan Af¬
rica is not in contradiction to the possible earlier onset of
HIV-1-related cognitive impairment: the more rapid pro¬
gression ofthe infection, in fact, may cause the death ofsev¬
eral subjects before they are able to reach the most severe
stages of cognitive deterioration24 (although this hypoth¬
esisneeds tobesubstantiatedby along-termfollow-upstudy).
It is important to emphasize that what we assessed
in the present investigation is the prevalence of the de¬
mentia syndrome according to 1CD-10 and DSM-HI-R,
which does notnecessarilycorrespond to thatofthe "AIDS
dementia complex"43 (since this last entity is defined also
by the presence of motor symptoms and signs and is di¬
agnosed by the exclusion of the etiological role of op¬
portunistic infections). We preferred, in fact, to rely on
a "syndromic" diagnosis that can be made by using a stan¬
dard algorithm based on a structured interview.
The results of the present cross-sectional phase ofthe
WHO study confirm that neurological impairment is a fre¬
quent occurrence in the symptomatic stages of HIV-1 in¬
fection, and thatfrontal release signs, impaired antisaccadic
eye movements, and muscle weakness are the most frequent
neurological abnormalities. As expected, the prevalence of
neurological signs was the highest among all serogroups
in the Bangkok center, where most subjects were intrave¬
nous drug users. We were able to confirm the significant
association reported by Stern et al10 between neurological
and neuropsychological impairment only in symptomatic
HIV-1-seropositive subjects, and in part of the centers,
whereas no association was found in asymptomatic sero¬
positive subjects in any center.
To our knowledge, our study was the first to adopt
operational diagnostic criteria for HIV-1-associated neu¬
rological disorders (it is important to note, in this connec¬
tion, the 100% consistencybetween the WHO and the AAN
diagnoses of myelopathy, predominantly sensory neur¬
opathy, and myopathy). The pattern ofneurological dis¬
orders was very similar across the centers, including the
two African sites, which may suggest a reconsideration of
the WHO Bangui clinical definition ofAIDS (that regards
neurological impairment as only a "minor" manifestation
of HIV-1 infection in Africa). The occurrence of a case of
inflammatorypolyneuropathy in an otherwise asymptom¬
atic HIV-1-seropositive subj ect is not surprising, since it
is known that this condition may be the first manifesta¬
tion ofHIV-1 infection.44 Less expected, and ofgreater in¬
terest, is the occurrence, among asymptomatic seroposi¬
tive subjects, of two cases ofpredominantly sensory neu¬
ropathy, a disorder that is regarded as rare in Centers of
Disease Control and Prevention groups II and III.16
The cross-sectional phase of the WHO study will now
befollowedbyalongitudinal one. Thiswillapproachthekey
issuesofthe evolutionofHIV- 1-associatedcognitive impair¬
ment and ofthe relationship between this evolution and the
immunological, medical, neurological, andpsychopathologi-
cal aspects of the infection. Moreover, it will verify the ex¬
istence of a continuity between mild cognitive abnormali¬
ties that possibly occur in the asymptomatic phases of the
infection and the subsequent development of dementia.
Accepted for publication December 29, 1992.
